planting.note: Plant acorns in fall in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, average to lean, sandy or loamy soil in full sun; Turkey oak is one of the fastest-growing European oaks and adapts to poor, dry soils where other oaks fail. The acorns require two years to mature (unlike one-year oaks), so current-year acorns are only half-formed.

directions: Plant acorns in fall in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, average to lean, sandy or loamy soil in full sun; Turkey oak is a fast-growing, medium to large deciduous oak native to southern Europe and western Asia, producing deeply and variably lobed leaves that are more irregular and 'bristle-tipped' than English oak, and distinctive acorn cups covered in long, curled, mossy-looking scales that make the acorns one of the most ornamental in any oak. The acorns require two full growing seasons to mature — a characteristic of the red oak group. Turkey oak is one of the fastest-growing and most drought-tolerant European oaks, often used in Europe for woodland planting on difficult, dry, sandy soils where English oak would fail. The fall color is variable — yellow to bronze. Widely planted as an ornamental in parks and large gardens for its vigorous growth and distinctive acorn cups. Be aware that in parts of the southeastern US, Turkey oak refers to the native Quercus laevis, a completely different species.

planting.note: Plant acorns immediately after collection in fall in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, average to lean, deep soil in full sun; sessile oak grows naturally on acidic, well-drained soils in hilly or upland areas — it prefers better-drained conditions than English oak (Q. robur) and is more common in the highland areas of Britain and western Europe.

directions: Plant acorns immediately after collection in fall in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, average to lean, deep soil in full sun; sessile oak is the co-dominant great oak of Europe alongside English oak (Q. robur), but distinguished by its stalkless (sessile) acorns held directly on the twigs (whereas English oak acorns hang on long stalks). The two species hybridize freely wherever their ranges overlap, producing intermediate forms. Sessile oak tends to grow on better-drained, more acidic, hillside soils while English oak occupies clay vales and wetlands. The 'ancient oakwoods' of Wales, Ireland, Scotland, and the western Highlands are typically sessile oak — including the famous Wistman's Wood on Dartmoor and the ancient semi-natural oakwoods of the Lake District. The wood is highly valued for fine furniture, flooring, and wine and whiskey barrel staves. An exceptionally long-lived tree reaching over 1,000 years. Provides the full suite of habitat value as English oak for British and Irish wildlife.

planting.note: Plant acorns immediately after collection in fall in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, average to fertile, deep soil in full sun; daimyo oak grows naturally in mountain forests and forest edges of China, Korea, and Japan, producing the largest leaves of any oak — up to 18 inches long — that create a dramatic, bold-textured specimen effect in the landscape.

directions: Plant acorns immediately after collection in fall in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, average to fertile, deep soil in full sun; daimyo oak (emperor oak) is the boldest-textured oak in the world, producing enormous, deeply lobed, paddle-shaped leaves that can reach 18 inches long and 10 inches wide — the largest leaves of any temperate-zone oak, creating a strikingly coarse, tropical-looking canopy texture completely unlike the small-leaved English oak. Native to mountain forests of China, Korea, and Japan, it is a relatively small to medium oak suitable for residential gardens where its dramatic foliage creates a bold statement. The leaves turn bronze to red-brown in fall and often persist on the tree into early winter before dropping (marcescent). Relatively slow-growing compared to other oaks. The species is the host plant for the silkworm Antheraea yamamai (the Japanese oak silkworm) which produces golden 'tussah' silk. An outstanding specimen tree for gardens where dramatic large-leaved texture is desired.

planting.note: Plant acorns immediately after collection in fall in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, deep loamy or sandy soil in full sun; Mongolian oak is an extremely cold-hardy Asian oak adapted to the severe winters of Manchuria, Mongolia, and Siberia, tolerating winter temperatures well below -30°F in its native range.

directions: Plant acorns immediately after collection in fall in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, deep loamy or sandy soil in full sun; Mongolian oak is one of the hardiest and most cold-tolerant of all oak species, native to the boreal and temperate forests of northeastern China (Manchuria), Mongolia, Korea, and the Russian Far East where it is a dominant tree species in continental climates with extreme winter cold and hot, dry summers. In North American gardens, it offers an excellent alternative to overcup oak (Q. lyrata) or white oak for extremely cold climates in zones 3–4 where most oaks would be marginal. The leaves are large, distinctly lobed with very broad, rounded lobes and are clustered at branch tips in a manner reminiscent of daimyo oak. Fall color is yellow to orange-brown. One of the most underused oaks for extremely cold-climate gardens where shade trees are limited.

planting.note: Plant container-grown trees in spring in well-drained, slightly acidic, moisture-retentive, fertile to average soil in full sun to partial shade; kobus magnolia does not like transplanting once established — plant in a permanent site. Trees grown from seed may take 10–30 years to bloom; grafted plants bloom in 3–5 years.

directions: Plant container-grown trees in spring in well-drained, slightly acidic, moisture-retentive, fertile to average soil in full sun to partial shade; kobus magnolia is a Japanese native and the parent of many important magnolia hybrids, producing abundant, slightly fragrant, white flowers with 9–12 petals in early spring before the leaves emerge — similar to star magnolia but on a larger, more tree-like plant. One of the most cold-hardy of the Asian magnolias, reliably hardy to zone 4. The Loebner magnolia hybrids (M. x loebneri), crosses of kobus with star magnolia, are particularly valued for combining cold hardiness with early, profuse blooming and are among the most reliable magnolias for northern gardens. Kobus itself is a stately, large specimen tree when old, eventually becoming a wide-spreading tree of considerable grace. The bark produces fragrant compounds used in Japanese temple incense.
